speaker: interviewee
text: But you're talking a time span of-- from '40-- late '41, '42 till '45, still snowing. When we got free, there was just this snow. I already melt-- melted in '45, so it had to be March, April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, Irvin Szames, roll three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Szames, you've been talking about your survival in the forests. I'd first like to ask if there's anything you would like to add that. You were last talking about gathering food and trying to gather food in a way so it wouldn't be detected. Would you like to talk about the end of your stay in the forest? I mean, if there isn't anything else you would like to add about that with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. About a half-- the following year, where it was the first year was a very rough year. The following year, we joined up. We-- we went into this one forest, which was already in the other side of Katowice, which is Katowice is around 40-- it's almost like 100 kilometers from where we were. When we went into that forest, this was a hangout. Or it was occupied by the-- the partisans. And this was in-- actually around '43 because it had to be. We already went through a season. So it had to be around there. And that's about timing that I can relate more or less and the time of the year because every incident had different timing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we met up with them, my older uncle, Sam, he went. He had enough of being with us family and all this. And he joined the partisans. Well, we all could join-- the older ones. But the girls and the kids were too young. And they didn't want them. But we lived in the same forest for quite awhile.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the partisans, they knew that we were Jews. And they didn't like us anymore than-- than the Germans or the Pollacks or the Ukrainians. A Jew down there was looked on bad, especially in this summer. However, they used to use us. And they used us to their advantage. In order for them to give us some food or let them stay-- let us stay in that forest, we had to-- had to perform certain things. And my uncle wanted to join them. So my Uncle Sam and I were the Guinea pigs for [? a ?] [? man. ?] We used to go out in formations. For instance, there was a train that had to be blown up. They used to put dynamite all around us. And you had a choice. Either you blow up the train. If you can't blow it up, you're going to get blown up because if you come back with mission unaccomplished, you were dead anyhow. And the Russians were like that. And there was no if, buts, or ands. So if they said, we want to know what's going on down there. We want this train blown up or this [RUSSIAN], the train station, blown up. Either you accomplished that or you had to get blown up with it. Or, if you come back not accomplished, you were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my uncle took me on a few missions, not because-- well, firstly he had to have-- he had to have it accomplished in order for us to live. Otherwise, they would have killed us. So the best one from the group that could do this stuff was me. I was fit. I was pretty capable in those days. I used to do the heavy hauling. And I used to do the climbing. I was little enough yet [? sprinting-- ?] what's the word I use? Well, capable enough and knowledgeable enough to climb over fences or climb over-- climb on trees. The [? job ?] of being eight years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Capable, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in small holes and also sensible enough to do what I had to do and get back. So this one-- this one time, we had to go into the-- to the-- to the train station. And they told my uncle-- I-- I-- I just know what he told me, where I had to go. So he came over. And he says, well, they told me that we have to do this and this-- blow up this train station and catch this train-- or else. Before-- they used to come. And the train station, when they came down with their soldiers, they used to have water refills. And I don't know if the American trains had the same thing. But you had those big logs of water towers. Like, the train would pull up. And a guy comes up and pulls the chain. And the water would pour in. Well, we had to blow up that-- that particular thing. So-- so my uncle was assigned to do it. And he said if we don't-- if I don't accomplish this mission, he's-- they're going to kill you guys. So Itzig has to go with me because in order to go through that, I'm too big. And we have to sneak in. It's outside. And he's small enough that he can carry everything. And-- and he promised my mom and he said that I'll come back, that he'll bring me back. He won't let me get killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with him. And we came-- came out. And I had to sneak out. And I was crawling. It must have been like-- well, like from here to-- to about I would say Main Street. And I had to crawl just like in distance. It was hard to see, but I knew where it is. I had to crawl over. And it was fields and long hay, go out of the woods while he was staying in the woods. And he told me what to do, where to lay down the stuff. And we accomplished the mission. I came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: He joined the partisans. And he and the group left. And we were allowed to stay in the forest. And we still didn't trust them. And it was a good thing because they didn't trust him. He says, don't trust the goy. And-- so we left. And there was-- there was two more families. And they got caught, we found out later. And we ran away from there and out of sight to a different forest. But all this was climate for Jew forests. And all of it was done at night. Life in the forest-- for instance, we would live in the snow. We'd dig out. And during the daytime, we would cover ourselves in this snow in the wintertime and-- and lay there. And underneath the snow, it was warm enough. You'd get warm. And during the day, you couldn't move. So had this-- you couldn't light fires-- not around there. If you lit one, you had to tunnel it so the smoke would go away. But anyhow, we stayed in the forest. I could talk on that for three, four hours. You know, every day had different stories. Every hour had different things happening, which we-- which a lot of times I don't like to talk about-- the different incidents. What we-- we ate practically nothing. We had to live from this. I still can-- my cousin, she lives in Boston and my cousin Rita that died. For instance, when we build a fire-- which was maybe once a month or less, what we used to do-- the reason we built a fire is to de-lice ourselves. And we'd stay or take off our clothes and hang it over because lice, in heat, will fall out. And they'll run away. And my cousins used to catch them and then put them over the fire and fry them. And then we would eat that because we didn't have any food. Or we'd take snow and melt on a fire and let it boil with that. We used to sneak out the-- the-- into the troughs. The Pollacks used to cook potatoes and sour milk and all this and feed the-- the hogs. And we used to steal whenever we could. I used to run in and grab some, and whatever we could grab, and bring it back. And we would nourish that for weeks. We didn't care about getting sour or whatever as long as you had something to put in-- wild berries and this kind of stuff. And that's what we lived on till we get freed by the-- by the Russians. And that was in early '45. So when we got freed, we finally [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] trusted them, freed. So we were still in [INAUDIBLE] and around.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said you were freed by them, how was it that you came into contact with Russian troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: When [? Sady ?] and I went out. Now, this is already a desperation of time. And we didn't have anything to eat. And we had to go out of the forest looking for food. So [? Sady ?] and I crawled out. And we laid-- we went into this cabbage field. And I was in the field. [? Sady ?] was laying halfway in the forest. And then, all of a sudden, the-- the Russian soldiers pulled up. Soldiers pulled up. We didn't see who they were. And they were close near me or almost like on top of me. And I was laying in the field. I just died there. And he seen the soldiers stop, so he ran back into the forest. And he told them that I got caught. So he and my family packed up, and they started running away to a different place because they figured, once they catch me, they'll force me to tell them where we were. So they started running away. Meanwhile, the guy ran over-- just one of the soldiers. And [INAUDIBLE], I guess he was their leader or whatever. Well, his horse stopped almost like on top of me. And I was laying down there. And they pulled me up. And I was scared. But I knew it wasn't a German. This was a different uniform. And I knew the Russian uniforms and this. He had the-- the gray with the pretty green tie with his [? shallat. ?] They were still wearing the long coats because it was still cold. And it was a Russian uniform. And of course, we speak-- spoke Ukrainian. And he was telling me that he's the Russian army, not to be afraid. And I told him I was a Jew, a Jude. [INAUDIBLE], you know. So-- so he called another guy over and-- from the circle. And [? Sady ?] seen all that, so he knew that I was going to be killed. And he grabbed the whole gang, and he started running away into a different forest. And meanwhile, the soldiers, they tried to convince me in all this. And I didn't trust them. So they said, you go ahead and-- and meet if you have family. I didn't tell him if I had any. But you go back where you-- you come from. And if you trust me, you can come out and bring them with you. And if you don't, just bring somebody with you. And you'll see that we're telling you the truth. We're the Russian army. We're not going to kill you. Anyhow, I-- they let me go. And I went back. And it took me almost a day or more to find them because they had already ran away. But we-- we sort of knew [? Sady ?] was-- he was sort of-- you know, we'll stay here for a few months. From here, we-- we've never been in this and this forest. So we'll go over here. We'll skip this one. And we'll go back. He was a foresighted type and my mother. And they always-- always used to plan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still live with this, with this-- what about if? What would happen if? And this was our life. If we get caught, what will we do? If we have to go, where will we go? And this was the type of planning that we all knew. And me being pretty much the-- the leader of all this, I knew that, if-- if I get separated, where would we meet? And this is the kind of life we lived. And I always knew what to do, the next step, as if.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so being afraid that I would turn them in, we used to wander around and wonder around. And finally we met up. And I told them what they told me. So then [? Sady ?] came out with me and he started questioning them and realized it was sure true. So we went out. And we went, then, back to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the city. It was a village. And this was already a bigger village, one of those villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- what was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, this was near Kiev. It was near Kiev, around there. We went into deep Poland already. Where was it-- not Katowice. I forgot the-- I think it was just outside Katowice. It was going towards Kiev. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can you tell us how it is you made your way to a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe the first one, you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- then from there-- after we were free, part of this-- and this is a history all by itself. The-- during the-- during the-- during the-- the-- the time where we were liberated, life in Europe, especially in Poland, the Russians hated the Ukraines. The Russians had as much animosity towards the Germans and the Ukraines as they-- and towards the Poles. And there was a point where they-- where a Jew was already looked up a lot better than the-- right after the war than the Ukraine because the Ukraine and the Russians were very-- they were fighting against each other there. So when we were freed, the Russians used to use us to try to track down some of the Ukraines and the Pollacks. So we had a-- we had a pretty good life for a little bit down there. And we used to catch a lot of the-- that we knew the Ukraines, so we used to catch them. And they'd hang them right there on the bazaar and the market. And you'd have truckloads. If we would go to some of our forest, our hiding out-- hideouts, and we'd find some of the-- of them, they used to bring them out and hang them. And if you recognized them, that's all you had to do is say, yeah. I remember them. And they'd hang them right away. And some of those women used to take the whips out of them. And to go to there on the bazaar-- on the market-- and not seeing five, 10 or so Ukraines hanging there-- or traitors, so to speak-- was an unusual sight. I mean, this is the type of our life we had. And then my mother used to bake rolls. I used to go to the train station and used to sell them in order to make some money. So from there, we went over to-- to Warsaw. We caught a train. And we went in. Right after the war, my-- my-- [? Sady ?] had a brother living in Columbus, Ohio-- an America. So our object was to get out of Poland and try to come to America. But you couldn't come to America from where we were. So our object is to go into deep Poland or go over to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went from where we were, around [INAUDIBLE], around that neck of the woods. We went over to [? Lwowna, ?] Bytom. Actually, I stayed in Bytom. When we went to Bytom, Poland-- from Lutsk to Bytom, that's where we were. We were [? always free ?] [? to ?] [? run ?] Lutsk. And from there, in Bytom we met up. I used to sell pop. And my Uncle Ben got married to Rita-- Rita Szames, [? Eric's ?] grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bytom, we lived down there for-- for a while. I started off a business by myself. And I was only-- you're talking '45. I was not quite 10. And I started selling lemonade. I started selling pop and-- on the-- on the year end. And because I was down there, I used to meet right across the street from where I was. There was the-- the Jewish pomach had an office down there. And they used to look for an office after the war. And what they tried to do is get the young children-- the orphans-- together. And I met them. And they used to come. And they had meetings down there. And they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about the Zionists?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Zionist movements, but it was basically, they were trying to catch Jewish survivors and-- to get them over to go to Palestine. And that was the movement. So I met up with them. And it was going on. And my sister and I-- and mother actually wanted us to-- to leave. I think my brother went with me, too. The three of us kids decided that-- that we'd better leave and survive. My mother always wanted us kids to survive, you know, because we didn't figure we're going to survive. So we signed up. And we left Poland. They took us into their organization. You had to become one of the kids. And you become oriented or told all about Palestine. I-- I just knew about Israel because of your prayers and all this, you know. But you didn't relate to Israel as a kid. But now we knew that this is where we have to go for all the Jews and-- to go back to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister and I, we-- we joined that movement. And it took us away from there. Adults couldn't go, only-- only orphans. And in order for us to go, we actually told them that we were orphans. And my parents-- my mother wanted us to-- to to get away from there. So we told them we were orphans. And they signed us up. So we joined-- we had to join that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we joined a kibbutz called the HaOgen Dror. You had to join a kibbutz. You-- you had your choice, at that time. And there you had Batar. No, Mizrahi wasn't there yet. Mizrahi was in Germany. But you had Yidi Batar or you had Dror. Those are the two organizations that you could join in Bytom. And they actually worked out of one building. And there wasn't that much. We were-- the difference between those, at least in my knowledge, was one was worked the land and defend it. The other one was fight for the land and-- and-- and work it. I think this is the biggest difference that I was-- I was told between Dror. Maybe I'm wrong. But as a kid, that's the only thing that we knew. But I-- I didn't join that as faith because of that-- our job was to go to Palestine and-- and survive. America, we only knew that we had some rich relatives in America. But we-- this is still being in [? goles, ?] meaning in the wilderness. And going to Palestine was a-- was the thing to do to get away from the pogroms, to get away from all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow-- Fay and I, we joined this. And we had a-- they took us to-- actually, from cities. And I don't know all the places. But it took months. They took us from one place. And we had to go to-- Can you name the-- the camps that you were at or the cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- we were-- we're going on trains. We were still in houses. And we had to go through Hungary. We went through Czechoslovakia, went through Rome. And then they took us into Germany. And when we got to Germany, that was already winter of '45. Late-- the leaves was already falling. First camp we came in, and you know escaping from there to there was a-- a life story by itself. But when we got into Camp Aschau. Aschau was-- Aschau by Diebling. No, Kindersdorf, I'm sorry. Aschau was second. Kindersdorf-- Kindersdorf was a-- was a Hitlerjugend camp, was basically a church with a complete background. It was like a square. And there they used to be Hitlerjugends training. And they had their own farm-- a self-sustained type of a church. They had all the land in the back. You had your own cattle. You had your own sheep. You had your own everything right down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived in-- this was my first DP camp, DP camp, which means displaced persons. And it was basically for children. You had to be 16 or under in order to be in those DP camps. If you were older than that, you were considered, really, an old man. And life-- life for all of us DP kids or people, when we were 10, 11, we already lived an average lifetime what now I would venture to say a person who lived to their 50s and their 70s didn't experience a 10th of lifestyle that most of us DP kids experienced by the time we were nine and 10 years old or 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: The biggest majority were the 10 to 13, 14-year-olds. So we had a lot, all of us, in common. We were bonded very close. And we were very, very easy molded into service as with one goal. Our-- our daily routine was we would-- we had the training. We would eat. They fed us very good. We would eat in the morning. We would have the Jewish studies-- Hebrew, mostly conversational and religious studies. And in the afternoons after lunch, it was basically training to and teaching to the-- the occupational part of the land because we knew, when we go there as soldiers, we're not going there as visitors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were going through-- now, in retrospect-- this military type of training such as-- I don't have the pictures now but stick fighting, knife defending and throwing, wall climbing, how to take apart a rifle or a gun with your eyes closed, how to throw hand grenades, tear gas, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the basic defense. And a matter of fact, one of my friends and I somewhere-- if I don't have it, my sister has it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is when we went through the training, we had this Haganahs. Those guys are rough guys. All of those guys, you talk. And I still love judo shows or martial art shows. Everyone of them could pick up, throw a knife, throw it at you, catch it in the air, and turn it around and throw to right back to you. And they didn't spare. They didn't treat you like children. We were all adults. And when you took stick fighting and when you had to defend yourself, you better defend yourself or else you'd get hurt because the-- the basic philosophy of those days were, you survived. Now you've got to learn how to survive again after you liberate. So there was no holds barred in the training. When he threw a knife at you, either you caught it or you got killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the defense-- and there was some kids that got beat up. I have one of the boys-- a good friend of mine. And when the guy was hitting him, he put out his stick like that and just broke in two. And it went right in down here like a pair of horns. I've got a picture of that. And that will teach you. And believe me, you learn. So our mission basically, in DP camps, was to go to Palestine and conquer the ground. And we went from-- from Kindersdorf, I went to Aschau. No-- yeah, to Aschau. In Aschau, we joined in order to get in that here-- in order to do this, you-- we-- we joined this Mizrahi group. That was the second one. And Aschau was a camp that it used to be-- the whole campus was-- was laid Aschau by-- by Diebling. yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It laid under-- under a forest. It used to be a German ammunition factory site. And there was caves all underneath the floors. And there was all kinds of ammunition. And there we-- we learned how to-- how to shoot. We had almost everything. We used to go out-- as kids, we used to go out fishing. And we'd take dynamite or hand grenades or what have you. And you know, you're talking about a 10-year-old. And we would go in. After the war was-- was a good life because we were rebels without-- with a cause without a-- without a rule. We would go into-- especially us, the-- the Polish surviving kids, we sort of stuck together from the Ukrainians and others. And when we were-- when we went someplace, matter of fact, the Haganahs taught us. And we used to have-- our pants were sort of overlaid with-- with tie-- we tied down here. And the pants were sort of a wound afterwards. And we used to carry in there weapons like dynamite, hand grenades, and others. Almost every kid that-- that walked the street in Germany used to have stuff. Part of this was, we will not be taken to [? death ?] like we did. And we were taught how to defend ourselves. We were taught how to defend your land. And so when we went to any place we went, we had enough ammunition on us-- like dynamite and hand grenades and all this-- to blow an arsenal up. We were kind of rough. But this is the type of training we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from-- from Aschau, we went to Rosenheim, which went very well. All this was taking place all around Munich, on the outskirts. And we were always guided by the-- the Haganah. We didn't join the Batar because it was more excessive fighting. And they were fighting a lot of our own. If you were a Batarist and you belonged to Dror or Mizrahi or any other kibbutzim, they were fighting among themselves. And we just-- my sister and I didn't want to go through that. So what we did then is, basically, joined a different-- we went to the different DP camps trying to get faster towards Israel. We used those camps for a means to an end. And it's not that they transferred us. But we heard, for instance, when we were in Kindersdorf and we heard that, you know, if you go over to Aschau and join this-- this group down there, they might get transferred to Israel next month or earlier. So you left there. Can we join?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we come in. We come in. We want to join. It was easy to join as long as you got over there. So we would jump a train or whatever and we'd come in. And in those days, the-- the American army was in charge. We were in an American zone. And it was a lot easier. We tried to escape the Russian and the Polish area because we didn't want to have anything to do there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just take one second.
